title: Rules of Hooks — Call Order and No Conditionals
Extract Custom Hooks for Reusable Logic
Composition Over Prop Drilling
description:
Instead of passing props through 3+ layers of components, restructure using composition. Pass components as children or props so intermediate layers don't need to know about the data.

Bad:
```jsx
<Layout user={user}>
  <Sidebar user={user}>
    <UserMenu user={user} />
  </Sidebar>
</Layout>
```

Good:
```jsx
<Layout sidebar={<Sidebar><UserMenu user={user} /></Sidebar>}>
  {children}
</Layout>
```

The Layout and Sidebar no longer need to know about `user` at all.

title: Controlled vs Uncontrolled Components
description:
Controlled components derive their value from React state (`value` + `onChange`). Uncontrolled components use the DOM as the source of truth (`defaultValue` + `ref`). Default to controlled for forms that need validation, conditional logic, or shared state. Use uncontrolled for simple isolated inputs where you only need the value on submit.

Controlled:
```jsx
const [email, setEmail] = useState('');
<input value={email} onChange={e => setEmail(e.target.value)} />
```

Uncontrolled:
```jsx
const inputRef = useRef(null);
<input defaultValue="" ref={inputRef} />
// read inputRef.current.value on submit
```

title: Premature useMemo and useCallback
description:
Don't wrap every value in `useMemo` or every function in `useCallback` by default. Memoization has a cost — the hook comparison runs every render. It only helps when: (1) the value is passed to a `React.memo`-wrapped child, (2) the computation is genuinely expensive, or (3) the value is a dependency of another hook.

Bad:
```jsx
const label = useMemo(() => `Hello ${name}`, [name]); // trivial computation
const handleClick = useCallback(() => setOpen(true), []); // no memo'd child
```

Good — only memoize when it matters:
```jsx
const sorted = useMemo(() => items.sort(compareFn), [items]); // expensive
<MemoizedList onSelect={useCallback(fn, [deps])} /> // memo'd child
```

title: Avoid Premature React.memo
description:
Don't wrap components in `React.memo` without measuring first. Memo adds a shallow comparison on every render. If the component receives objects or functions as props (which are new references every render), memo provides zero benefit while adding overhead. Profile first, then memo components that: (1) render often with the same props, (2) are expensive to render, and (3) receive stable prop references.

Bad:
```jsx
export default React.memo(SmallStatelessLabel); // trivial render
```

Good:
```jsx
// Profile shows this list item re-renders 500 times with same props
export default React.memo(ExpensiveListItem);
```

title: Error Boundaries for Graceful Failure
Keys Must Be Stable, Unique, and Data-Derived
State Colocation — Keep State Close to Where It's Used
Derived State — Compute, Don't Sync
useRef for Mutable Values That Don't Trigger Renders
Suspense Boundaries for Async Loading
Context API Pitfall — Frequently Changing Values
description:
Every component consuming a context re-renders when the context value changes — even if it only reads a stable slice. Don't put frequently changing values (mouse position, animation frames, rapidly updating form state) in context.

Bad:
```jsx
<AppContext.Provider value={{ user, theme, mousePosition }}>
// Every mousemove re-renders ALL consumers
```

Good — split contexts by update frequency:
```jsx
<UserContext.Provider value={user}>
  <ThemeContext.Provider value={theme}>
    {children}
  </ThemeContext.Provider>
</UserContext.Provider>
```

For high-frequency values, use a ref-based subscription pattern or a dedicated state library.

title: One Component Per File
description:
Each React component gets its own file, named to match the component. Co-locate related files (styles, tests, types) in the same directory. Small helper components used only by one parent can live in the same file, but if they grow or get reused, extract them.

```
Button/
  Button.tsx        // component
  Button.test.tsx   // tests
  Button.module.css // styles
  index.ts          // re-export
```

This makes components easy to find, move, and delete. A file with 5 components is a file where no one can find anything.

title: Event Handler Naming Conventions
useEffect as Event Handler
Prefer Enums and Union Types Over Boolean Props
description:
When a component has mutually exclusive modes, use a union-typed prop instead of multiple booleans. This prevents impossible states and is self-documenting.

Bad:
```jsx
<Button isPrimary isOutlined isGhost /> // which one wins?
```

Good:
```jsx
<Button variant="primary" />
<Button variant="outlined" />
<Button variant="ghost" />
// type Variant = 'primary' | 'outlined' | 'ghost'
```

This extends to size, status, and any prop where only one value should apply at a time.

title: Fetching Data in useEffect Without Cleanup
